---
title: "Data Club - Curate a Personal Met Viewing Expience"
format: html
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd
```

```{python}
df_art = pd.read_csv("C:/Users/court/Downloads/Data_Clubs_Project_1/archive/MetObjects.csv")
```

```{python}
df_art.describe()
```

---

# Step 1: Define general Periods

Let's Start by creating classifications for the art so we can begain to parse the "vibe" / tone for the art.

# Ancient Art:
Before 800 BCE (this is usually classified by geography)
(Topics: Sacred ritual, power of rulers, the afterlife, foundational storytelling in visual form.)

# Art Periods:
Greek/Roman: 800 BCE - 400 CE
(Topics: Human idealism (Greek), civic power & realism (Roman), mythology, perfected form.)

# Medieval: 400 - 1350
(Topics: The glory of God, religious narrative, hierarchy over realism, symbolism.)
# (Movements within: Byzantine, Romanesque, Gothic. ) # Note to self maybe break into geogrpahy for these? 

# Renaissance: 1350 - 1600
(Topics: Humanism, rediscovery of classical art, scientific perspective, individual genius.)
# (Movements within: Early Renaissance, High Renaissance, Mannerism.) # Note to self break by time

# Baroque: 1600 - 1750
(Topics: Drama, tension, grandeur, theatrical light, engaging the senses.)
# *(Movements within: Rococo [late Baroque, ~1720-1760])*

# Neoclassical: 1750 - 1800
(Topics: Civic virtue, logic, order, classical models from Ancient Greece/Rome.)

# Romantic: 1800 -1865
(Topics: Emotion, the sublime in nature, individualism, dramatic rebellion.)


# Art Movements:
Impressionism: 1865 - 1885
(Topics: Modern life, fleeting light, sensory impression, painting en plein air.)

# Post-Impressionism: 1885 - 1910
(Topics: Symbolic color, structural form, emotional and personal expression.)
(Related: Fauvism, Expressionism.)

# Modern: 1910 - 1960
(Topics: Experimentation, abstraction, rejecting tradition, art about art itself.)
(Key Movements: Cubism, Futurism, Dada, Surrealism, Abstract Expressionism.)

# Contemporary: 1960 - Now
(Topics: Conceptualism, identity politics, globalization, questioning boundaries.)
(Key Movements: Pop Art, Minimalism, Performance Art, Installation, Postmodernism.)

---

# Step 2: Define Categories for Viewing


Let's create some options for a viewers..

# Step 2.1: Personality Archetypes (Broad Filter)
These are the foundational lenses through which a user sees the world.
---

## The Classicist: 

Values order, balance, clarity, and timeless ideals. Seeks harmony and perfected form.

Potential Art Match: Greek/Roman, Renaissance, Neoclassical.


## The Romantic: 
Driven by emotion, intuition, and imagination. Drawn to the dramatic, the mysterious, and the sublime power of nature.

Potential Art Match: Romantic, Baroque, Gothic, some Surrealism.


## The Realist: Pragmatic, observant, and grounded. Values truth, authenticity, and the unvarnished moment.

Potential Art Match: Realism, Social Realism, some Dutch Golden Age.

## The Innovator: 
Experimental, abstract, and intellectual. Challenges conventions and explores ideas, form, and perception itself.

Potential Art Match: Cubism, Futurism, Abstract Expressionism, much of Modern art.

## The Symbolist: 
Seeks deeper meaning, metaphor, and the spiritual. Drawn to mystery, dreams, and coded narratives.

Potential Art Match: Medieval, Symbolist movement, Surrealism, much of Contemporary conceptual art.

## The Hedonist: Sensual, decorative, and attuned to pleasure, beauty, and the elegance of surface.

Potential Art Match: Rococo, Impressionism, some Post-Impressionism (like Renoir), Art Nouveau.
---

# Step 2.2: Moods / Vibes (The Specific Filter)
These are the immediate emotional or atmospheric states.
---
# A. Energy & Composition
## Dynamic & Dramatic: 
Diagonal lines, intense contrast, theatrical movement.
Periods: Baroque, Romantic, Futurism.
## Calm & Balanced: 
Horizontal lines, symmetrical composition, serene subjects.
Periods: Renaissance, Neoclassical, Minimalism.
## Chaotic & Raw: 
Energetic brushwork, fragmented forms, intense emotion.
Periods: Expressionism, Abstract Expressionism, some Contemporary.
---
# B. Color & Light
## Warm & Golden: 
Rich golds, deep reds, chiaroscuro (strong light/shadow).
Periods: Renaissance, Baroque, Romantic.
## Cool & Ethereal:
Icy blues, silvery light, misty atmospheres.
Periods: Gothic, Romantic (Caspar David Friedrich), some Surrealism.
## Vibrant & Unnatural: 
Bold, non-representational color used for emotional impact.
Periods: Fauvism, Post-Impressionism (Van Gogh), Pop Art.
## Muted & Naturalistic: 
Earth tones, "real" light, subtle gradations.
Periods: Realism, Impressionism (overcast days), Dutch Golden Age.
---
# C. Subject & Concept
## The Human Figure:
Idealized, expressive, or deconstructed.
Periods: Greek/Roman, Renaissance, Expressionism, Cubism.
## Nature & Landscape: 
Sublime, pastoral, or scientifically observed.
Periods: Romantic, Impressionism, Realism, Hudson River School.
## The Inner World:
Dreams, the subconscious, spiritual visions.
Periods: Surrealism, Symbolism, Medieval visionary art.
## Urban & Modern Life
The city, technology, leisure, social critique.
Periods: Impressionism, Realism, Ashcan School, Futurism, Pop Art.
## The Abstract Idea: 
Pure form, color, geometry, or conceptual premise.
Periods: Abstract Expressionism, Minimalism, Conceptual Art.
---
# D. Texture & Surface
## Smooth & Polished: 
Perfect marble, flawless glaze, invisible brushwork.
Periods: Greek/Roman, Renaissance, Neoclassical.
## Textured & Painterly
Visible brushstrokes, thick impasto, evidence of the artist's hand.
Periods: Impressionism, Post-Impressionism, Abstract Expressionism.
## Ornate & Decorative
Intricate detail, pattern, gilt, and curvature.
Periods: Rococo, Gothic, Art Nouveau.
----

# Step 3: Let's start by normalizing these dates!

In a previous class I normailized dates, this section is open to interpretation, and the way I'll do this is proably to formulaic for most art historians, but this isn't that serious of a project/objective so it should be fine. 

If an Object Begin Date does not Equal Object End Date I'll take the midpoint of the dates. 


```{python}

df_art['year'] = df_art.apply(
    lambda row: row['Object Begin Date'] if row['Object Begin Date'] == row['Object End Date']
    else (row['Object Begin Date'] + row['Object End Date']) / 2, 
    axis=1
)

df_art

```

```{python}
import numpy as np
df_art['year'] = df_art['year'].round(0)

df_art
```

# Step 4" Cateogize dates into periods/movements
Lets start by putting dates into broad time period cateogies and movement cateogries. 

Using past art history knowledge I'll combine date and Country (when country data is avalible) to get more specific movements. I know there are plenty of Geolocation tags but for the sake of my sanity I'll pair it down to just conuntry alone when determining movement. 

```{python}
def period_movement(year, country=None):

    year_float = float(year)
    country_str = str(country).lower() if country and pd.notna(country) else ""
    
    # Ancient Art: Before 800 BCE
    if year_float < -800:
        return ("Ancient Art", "Ancient (Pre-800 BCE)")
    
    # Greek/Roman: 800 BCE - 400 CE
    elif -800 <= year_float <= 400:
        if year_float < 0:
            return ("Greek/Roman", "Greek")
        else:
            return ("Greek/Roman", "Roman")
    
    # Medieval: 400 - 1350
    elif 400 < year_float <= 1350:
        if 'byzant' in country_str or 'turkey' in country_str or 'constantinople' in country_str:
            return ("Medieval", "Byzantine")
        elif 1000 <= year_float <= 1200 and ('france' in country_str or 'germany' in country_str or 'italy' in country_str):
            return ("Medieval", "Romanesque")
        elif 1150 <= year_float <= 1350 and ('france' in country_str or 'england' in country_str or 'germany' in country_str):
            return ("Medieval", "Gothic")
        else:
            return ("Medieval", "Medieval")
    
    # Renaissance: 1350 - 1600
    elif 1350 < year_float <= 1600:
        if 1350 < year_float <= 1490:
            movement = "Early Renaissance"
        elif 1490 < year_float <= 1520:
            movement = "High Renaissance"
        else:
            movement = "Mannerism"
        
        # Add country context if available
        if country_str:
            if 'italy' in country_str or 'florence' in country_str or 'venice' in country_str:
                movement = f"Italian {movement}"
            elif 'northern' in country_str or 'netherland' in country_str or 'flemish' in country_str:
                movement = f"Northern {movement}"
        
        return ("Renaissance", movement)
    
    # Baroque: 1600 - 1750
    elif 1600 < year_float <= 1750:
        if 1720 <= year_float <= 1760 and ('france' in country_str or 'germany' in country_str):
            return ("Baroque", "Rococo")
        else:
            if 'italy' in country_str:
                return ("Baroque", "Italian Baroque")
            elif 'spain' in country_str:
                return ("Baroque", "Spanish Baroque")
            elif 'flemish' in country_str or 'netherland' in country_str:
                return ("Baroque", "Flemish Baroque")
            else:
                return ("Baroque", "Baroque")
    
    # Neoclassical: 1750 - 1800
    elif 1750 < year_float <= 1800:
        return ("Neoclassical", "Neoclassical")
    
    # Romantic: 1800 - 1865
    elif 1800 < year_float <= 1865:
        if 'germany' in country_str:
            return ("Romantic", "German Romanticism")
        elif 'england' in country_str or 'british' in country_str:
            return ("Romantic", "British Romanticism")
        elif 'france' in country_str:
            return ("Romantic", "French Romanticism")
        else:
            return ("Romantic", "Romanticism")
    
    # Impressionism: 1865 - 1885
    elif 1865 < year_float <= 1885:
        return ("Impressionism", "Impressionism")
    
    # Post-Impressionism: 1885 - 1910
    elif 1885 < year_float <= 1910:
        if 'france' in country_str and year_float >= 1900:
            return ("Post-Impressionism", "Fauvism")
        elif 'germany' in country_str or 'austria' in country_str:
            return ("Post-Impressionism", "Expressionism")
        else:
            return ("Post-Impressionism", "Post-Impressionism")
    
    # Modern: 1910 - 1960
    elif 1910 < year_float <= 1960:
        if 1910 < year_float <= 1925:
            if 'france' in country_str or 'spain' in country_str:
                return ("Modern", "Cubism")
            elif 'italy' in country_str:
                return ("Modern", "Futurism")
            elif 'switzerland' in country_str or 'germany' in country_str:
                return ("Modern", "Dada")
            else:
                return ("Modern", "Early Modernism")
        
        elif 1925 < year_float <= 1945:
            if 'spain' in country_str or 'france' in country_str:
                return ("Modern", "Surrealism")
            elif 'germany' in country_str or 'norway' in country_str:
                return ("Modern", "Expressionism")
            else:
                return ("Modern", "Surrealism/Expressionism")
        
        else:  # 1945 - 1960
            if 'united states' in country_str or 'usa' in country_str or 'america' in country_str:
                return ("Modern", "Abstract Expressionism")
            else:
                return ("Modern", "Abstract Expressionism/Informel")
    
    # Contemporary: 1960 - Now
    else:  
        if 1960 < year_float <= 1980:
            if 'united states' in country_str or 'britain' in country_str:
                return ("Contemporary", "Pop Art")
            elif 'united states' in country_str:
                return ("Contemporary", "Minimalism")
            else:
                return ("Contemporary", "Pop Art/Minimalism")
        
        elif 1980 < year_float <= 2000:
            return ("Contemporary", "Postmodernism")
        
        else:  # 2000 - Now
            return ("Contemporary", "Contemporary")

def add_period(df, year_column='year', country_column='Country'):
    
    classifications = df.apply(
        lambda row: period_movement(
            row[year_column], 
            row[country_column] if country_column in df.columns else None
        ), 
        axis=1
    )
    
    df['art_period'] = classifications.apply(lambda x: x[0])
    df['art_movement'] = classifications.apply(lambda x: x[1])
    
    return df

df_art = add_period(df_art)
```


# Step 5: Lets Update Broad period catoegies with new naming conventions: 

## Ancient Art: 
art_period: "Ancient Art"
#art_movement: "Ancient (Pre-800 BCE)"
Before 800 BCE (this is usually classified by geography)

## Art Periods: Greek/Roman: 800 BCE - 400 CE
art_period: "Greek/Roman"
art_movement: "Greek" (for BCE dates) or "Roman" (for CE dates)

## Medieval: 400 - 1350
art_period: "Medieval"
art_movement: "Medieval", "Byzantine", "Romanesque", or "Gothic"

## Renaissance: 1350 - 1600
art_period: "Renaissance"
art_movement: "Early Renaissance", "High Renaissance", "Mannerism", 
    "Italian Early Renaissance", "Northern Renaissance", etc.

## Baroque: 1600 - 1750
art_period: "Baroque"
art_movement: "Baroque", "Italian Baroque", "Spanish Baroque", 
    "Flemish Baroque", "Rococo"

## Neoclassical: 1750 - 1800
art_period: "Neoclassical"
art_movement: "Neoclassical"

## Romantic: 1800 -1850
art_period: "Romantic"
art_movement: "Romanticism", "German Romanticism", 
     "British Romanticism", "French Romanticism"

## Realistic: 1850 - 1900 
art_period: "Realistic" 
art_movement: "Realism" (Note: using "Realistic" period but "Realism" movement)
*** Added this period to get an overall better theme for this period the movement catoegory will be used to break this down further. 

## Impressionism: 1865 - 1885
art_period: "Impressionism"
art_movement: "Impressionism"

## Post-Impressionism: 1885 - 1910
art_period: "Post-Impressionism"
art_movement: "Post-Impressionism", "Fauvism", "Expressionism"

## Modern: 1910 - 1960
art_period: "Modern"
art_movement: "Early Modernism", "Cubism", "Futurism", "Dada", 
    "Surrealism", "Expressionism", "Abstract Expressionism"

## Contemporary: 1960 - Now
art_period: "Contemporary"
art_movement: "Pop Art", "Minimalism", "Postmodernism", 
    "Contemporary", "Pop Art/Minimalism"
---

# Step 6: Defining style/mood/vibe words

```{python}

df_art['Classification'] = df_art['Classification'].str.extract(r'^([^|]*)')

u_classifications = df_art['Classification'].value_counts()
u_classifications.columns = ['Classification', 'Count']
u_classifications
```

```{python}

u_Department = df_art['Department'].value_counts()
u_Department.columns = ['Department', 'Count']
u_Department
```

Exported both of these as CSVs 

Gave results to Deepseek AI so it come up with a third option for filters: 

## Category 1: Painting & Color Arts
What you'll see: Canvases, pigments, visible brushwork, color as expression

Paintings (11,049)

Paintings-Decorative (99)

Paintings-Panels (39)

Paintings-Fresco (19)

Paintings-Canvas (2)

Paintings-Frescoes (1)

Paintings-Icons (1)

Related Departments: European Paintings, American Paintings and Sculpture, Modern and Contemporary Art

## Category 2: Sculpture & 3D Form
What you'll see: Figures, forms, materials carved/cast/modeled in space

Sculpture (4,494)

Sculpture-Architectural (770)

Sculpture-Bronze (681)

Sculpture-Miniature (296)

Sculpture-Stone (276)

Sculpture-Wood (238)

Sculpture-Reproductions (13)

Sculpture-Conceptual Art (3)

Sculpture-Marble (2)

Sculpture-Ceramics (2)

Related Departments: European Sculpture and Decorative Arts, Greek and Roman Art, Modern and Contemporary Art

## Category 3: Works on Paper
What you'll see: Drawings, prints, watercolors - intimate, immediate works

Prints (105,653)

Drawings (29,291)

Works on Paper-Drawings (107)

Pastels & Oil Sketches on Paper (29)

Works on Paper-Engravings (29)

Cut Paper (810)

Paper-Drawings (132)

Illustrated Books (597)

Stencils (569)

Related Departments: Drawings and Prints, Photographs, The Libraries

## Category 4: Photography & Lens-Based
What you'll see: Captured moments, documentary, artistic photography

Photographs (38,200)

Negatives (6,483)

Transparencies (209)

Paper-Photographs (11)

Related Departments: Photographs, Modern and Contemporary Art

## Category 5: Textiles & Fabric Arts
What you'll see: Woven, embroidered, dyed fabrics, clothing, tapestries

Textiles-Woven (11,058)

Textiles-Laces (4,980)

Textiles-Embroidered (4,160)

Textiles (3,080)

Textiles-Printed (2,560)

Textiles-Trimmings (1,950)

Textiles-Velvets (1,443)

Textiles-Tapestries (643)

Textiles-Rugs (621)

Textiles-Costumes (575)

Related Departments: Costume Institute, Arts of Africa/Oceania/Americas, Asian Art

## Category 6: Ceramics & Clay
What you'll see: Pots, vessels, porcelain, earthenware, fired clay objects

Ceramics (13,332)

Ceramics-Porcelain (4,104)

Ceramics-Pottery (3,528)

Ceramics-Containers (1,086)

Ceramics-Vessels (908)

Ceramics-Porcelain-Export (787)

Ceramics-Tiles (361)

Ceramics-Sculpture (415)

Ceramics-Implements (265)

Related Departments: Asian Art, European Sculpture and Decorative Arts, Egyptian Art

## Category 7: Metal Arts
What you'll see: Jewelry, armor, silverware, bronze work, weapons

Metalwork-Silver (3,974)

Metalwork (2,372)

Gold and Silver (1,950)

Metal-Ornaments (1,896)

Metalwork-Iron (1,262)

Silver (1,156)

Metalwork-Gilt Bronze (886)

Metalwork-Gold and Platinum (699)

Metalwork-Copper alloy (587)

Swords (1,080)

Arms and Armor (35)

Related Departments: Arms and Armor, European Sculpture and Decorative Arts, Islamic Art

## Category 8: Glass & Transparency
What you'll see: Stained glass, vessels, beads, translucent objects

Glass (8,846)

Glass-Stained (1,066)

Glass-Vessels (854)

Glass-Ornaments (134)

Glass-Painted (108)

Related Departments: European Sculpture and Decorative Arts, Medieval Art, The Cloisters

## Category 9: Wood & Organic Materials
What you'll see: Carved wood, furniture, natural materials, organic forms

Furniture (1,826)

Wood-Sculpture (1,670)

Woodwork-Furniture (1,415)

Woodwork (713)

Wood-Implements (684)

Wood-Containers (199)

Wood-Musical Instruments (184)

Wood-Furniture (94)

Woodwork-Architectural (97)

Related Departments: American Decorative Arts, European Sculpture and Decorative Arts, Arts of Africa/Oceania/Americas

## Category 10: Precious & Miniature Arts
What you'll see: Small-scale luxury items, jewelry, gems, intricate work

Jewelry (2,415)

Gems (1,613)

Jade (1,657)

Medals and Plaquettes (1,681)

Netsuke (941)

Snuff Bottles (629)

Inrō (539)

Ojime (151)

Miniatures (257)

Related Departments: Asian Art, Islamic Art, European Sculpture and Decorative Arts

## Category 11: Books & Manuscripts
What you'll see: Illuminated manuscripts, books, calligraphy, documents

Books (20,441)

Albums (1,893)

Codices (1,400)

Manuscripts and Illuminations (343)

Calligraphy (332)

Manuscripts (76)

Books & Manuscripts (19)

Related Departments: The Libraries, Medieval Art, Islamic Art

## Category 12: Ancient & Archaeological
What you'll see: Artifacts, tablets, seals, objects from ancient civilizations

Terracottas (1,763)

Clay-Tablets-Inscribed (536)

Stone-Cylinder Seals (446)

Coins (1,127)

Papyrus (204)

Cesnola Inscriptions (189)

Tomb Pottery (303)

Related Departments: Egyptian Art, Ancient Near Eastern Art, Greek and Roman Art

## Category 13: Contemporary & Mixed Media
What you'll see: Modern techniques, installations, conceptual works, new media

Installations (47)

Variable Media (77)

Collages (65)

Assemblages (7)

Ephemera (855)

Posters (167)

Miscellaneous (737)

Related Departments: Modern and Contemporary Art, Photographs

## Category 14: Musical Instruments
What you'll see: Historical instruments from around the world

Musical Instruments (5,303)

Chordophone-Lute-plucked-fretted (283)

Aerophone-Blow Hole-side-blown flute (217)

Idiophone-Struck-bell-clapper (217)

Idiophone-Shaken-rattle (213)

Related Department: Musical Instruments

## Category 15: Architecture & Space
What you'll see: Architectural elements, models, fragments, spatial works

Architecture (170)

Ornament & Architecture (354)

Architectural Models (12)

Screens (171)

Frames (560)

Wallpaper (51)

Related Departments: Medieval Art, The Cloisters, European Sculpture and Decorative Arts
---

FUTURE STEPS!!!


STREAM LIT

Then lets design how people will select imputs to view:
- I'm thinking you could have a giant word bank one for personality and one for mood/vibe. 
- then the proportion of verbs selected will determine proportion of those styles returned to users.
- Have the results return the pictures/images of a couple examples.

OPTIONAL 
- I could add a map of the met muesem using the Is Highlight feature I could point out how many a viewer could see, if met website is well designed you could use department to color code a map? 
- Thats going to be hard tho... 
- Check this link out: https://maps.metmuseum.org/?_gl=1%2A15ppzta%2A_gcl_au%2AMjEzNzU2NjAyLjE3NjcxMzc3MTE.%2A_ga%2AMTIxMDAzNzI2Ny4xNzY3MTM3NzEx%2A_ga_Y0W8DGNBTB%2AczE3NjcxMzc3MTAkbzEkZzAkdDE3NjcxMzc3MTUkajU1JGwwJGgw&floor=1&lang=en-GB#17.29/40.779573/-73.963382/-61
